flashcards.back: The process of finding a specific element within a collection of data. Common methods: Linear Search (O(n)) and Binary Search (O(log n)).
The process of arranging elements in a specific order (ascending or descending). Common methods: Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort.
Sequentially checks each element in the list until the target is found or the end is reached. Works on unsorted and sorted data. Time: O(n).
Compares target with middle element, eliminates half the search space each time. Requires sorted data. Time: O(log n).
Repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if in wrong order. Largest elements 'bubble up' to the end. Time: O(n^2) average, O(n) best.
Finds the minimum element from unsorted part and places it at the beginning. Time: O(n^2) in all cases.
Builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each element into its correct position. Time: O(n^2) average, O(n) best.
A sorting algorithm that preserves the relative order of equal elements. Bubble Sort and Insertion Sort are stable. Selection Sort is not stable.

flashcards.front: What is an in-place sorting algorithm?
flashcards.back: A sorting algorithm that uses O(1) extra memory. Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, and Insertion Sort are all in-place algorithms.

flashcards.front: When should you use binary search vs linear search?
flashcards.back: Use binary search when data is sorted (O(log n)). Use linear search when data is unsorted or the list is small (O(n)).
